fact: In the Northern Hemisphere, the sun sets so early that by 4:30 PM it's already getting dark in many places - like nature's way of saying 'time for hot chocolate!'
Squirrels are frantically burying their final nuts around this time, creating underground treasure maps they'll probably forget by spring.
The Geminids meteor shower peaks right around December 12, shooting up to 120 colorful meteors per hour across the night sky like cosmic fireworks.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, December 12 brings blazing summer heat while the north freezes - talk about opposite day!
Evergreen trees like pine and spruce are pumping out extra sap right now to prevent their branches from freezing and snapping off.
The moon around December 12 appears extra bright and crisp because cold winter air has less moisture to blur its glow.
Arctic foxes are sporting their fluffiest white winter coats by this date, making them look like walking snowballs with eyes.
December 12 falls smack in the middle of Sagittarius season, when people born under this zodiac sign are supposedly extra adventurous and ready to explore.
In northern regions, the ground is freezing so hard that earthworms burrow down 6 feet deep to avoid becoming worm popsicles.
The winter solstice is only 9 days away from December 12, meaning each day loses about 2 more minutes of sunlight in the north.
Deciduous trees have been completely bare for weeks by this date, but their roots are still secretly growing underground in the warmer soil.
